quotes.quote: Galaxies: jets -- Galaxies: active -- Galaxies: individual: 3C 84 (NGC 1275) -- Techniques: interferometric -- Techniques: high angular resolution \ is a peculiar Seyfert 1.5-type radio galaxy 2006A&A...455..773V . It is located relatively nearby, at a distance of 76.9 Mpc ( z = 0.0176) 1992ApJS...83...29S We assume cold dark matter cosmology with H _ 0 = 71 km/s/Mpc, _ = 0.73, and _M = 0.27. , and harbours a central super massive black hole (SMBH) of M_ BH 9 10^8 M_ 2013MNRAS.429.2315S . This makes \ a prime target to pinpoint the location of the SMBH and to study the magnetic field in the very inner jet region for this enigmatic radio galaxy.

The analysis of the core shift reveals that the jet apex is located north-west of the VLBI core at 86 GHz, displaced by as, with the distance from the core as a function of the frequency, following Eq. eq:kr . With the detected core shift and the possible location of the jet apex north of the 86 GHz VLBI core, the east-west oriented VLBI core structure (which is also seen in the 22 GHz RadioAstron map) appears less likely to be the physical origin of the jet.

At a de-projected distance of R _s ( as) from the jet apex, the magnetic field topology is not purely poloidal; a mixed poloidal-toroidal configuration is suggested. This points towards a stratified combination of the BP and BZ models (acting in parallel) or towards an alteration to the initial magnetic field configuration due to some internal physical jet processes acting farther downstream (e.g. developing shocks and/or instabilities). We measure the magnetic field to be in the range B_0= G at the jet apex.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes NGC 1275 with deep Chandra HETG data, modeling its composite AGN X-ray source, molecular-gas absorption tension, missing winds, and iron-K fluorescence.

quotes.quote: NGC 1275 is the Brightest Cluster Galaxy (BCG) in the Perseus cluster and hosts the active galactic nucleus (AGN) that is heating the central 100 kpc of the intracluster medium (ICM) atmosphere via a regulated feedback loop. Here we use a deep (490 ks) Cycle-19 Chandra High-Energy Transmission Grating (HETG) observation of NGC 1275 to study the anatomy of this AGN. The X-ray continuum is adequately described by an unabsorbed power-law with photon index 1.9 , creating strong tension with the detected column of molecular gas seen via HCN and HCO ^+ line absorption against the parsec-scale core/jet.

This tension is resolved if we permit a composite X-ray source; allowing a column of N_H 8 10^ 22 to cover 15 of the X-ray emitter does produce a significant improvement in the statistical quality of the spectral fit. We suggest that the dominant unabsorbed component corresponds to the accretion disk corona, and the sub-dominant X-ray component is the jet working surface and/or jet cocoon that is expanding into clumpy molecular gas. We suggest that this may be a common occurence in BCG-AGN.

We conduct a search for photoionized absorbers/winds and fail to detect such a component, ruling out columns and ionization parameters often seen in many other Seyfert galaxies. We detect the 6.4 keV iron-K fluorescence line seen previously by XMM-Newton and Hitomi .

sources.summary: The paper studies NGC 1275 as a nearby Perseus radio galaxy, using VLBI to locate its jet apex, map spectral-index structure, and infer magnetic fields.

quotes.quote: In this work we study the prominent and nearby radio galaxy \ (NGC 1275) with 15, 43, and 86 GHz quasi-simultaneous VLBI observations. From these we determine the jet apex to be located 83 7 as ( 0.028-0.11 pc) upstream of the 86 GHz VLBI core, applying a two dimensional cross-correlation analysis. A byproduct of this analysis are spectral index maps, in which we identify a robust spectral index gradient in the north-south direction, for the first time at such high resolution, for the 43-86 GHz pair.

NGC 1275 is the central radio galaxy of the Perseus cluster. It harbours , which is one of the brightest radio galaxies in the northern sky, making it a suitable target for high spatial resolution imaging with centimetre- and millimetre-VLBI. From radio to rays, the source shows prominent flux variability (see Fig. fig:flux ). Its relation to the structural variability detected in the VLBI images is a topic of intense study. The first VLBI images of \ were published in the early 1950s Baade54 .

sources.summary: The paper studies the parsec-scale jet of 3C 84/NGC 1275, measuring VLBI component kinematics, frequency-dependent jet width, and spectral-index structure over two decades.

quotes.quote: Galaxies: jets -- Galaxies: active -- Galaxies: individual: 3C 84 (NGC 1275) -- Techniques: interferometric -- Techniques: high angular resolution Studying the kinematics of AGN jets at the sub-parsec scale has only recently been made possible by the advancement of technology utilised in centimetre- and millimetre very-long-baseline interferometry (VLBI). Still, only a few sources provide the necessary criteria required to resolve the complex, physical phenomena at play. The best suited candidate AGN should be nearby, harbour a super massive black hole, which powers a jet, preferably transversely resolved, and which is oriented with respect to the observer at a moderately large viewing angle to avoid strong Doppler beaming effects. The existence of measurements over a long timeline at high cadence, and at different frequencies, is also advantageous. , the radio source associated with the central galaxy NGC 1275 in the Perseus cluster Ho97 , fulfils all these criteria. The prominent type 2 radio-galaxy is indeed very nearby ( D_L = 78.9 Mpc, z=0.0176 , Strauss92 ) We assume cold dark matter cosmology with H_0 = 67.8 km/s/Mpc, _ = 0.692 , and _M=0.308 Planck16 . and harbours a super massive black hole (M _ BH 9 10^8 M _ , Scharwaechter13 ). A multitude of epochs at numerous frequencies is available in the literature, as the source has been studied in detail over more than half a century Baade54, Walker94, Dhawan98, Walker00, Suzuki12, Nagai14, Dutson14, Giovannini18, Kim19, Paraschos21 . Two jets emanate from the central engine Vermeulen94, Walker94, Fujita17 ; one moves southward and is very prominent, while the northern one is much fainter and is obscured by free-free absorption on milli-arcsecond (mas) scales Wajima20 .

The fact that we are able to cross-identify jet components in 3C 84 at two frequencies and are able to describe the motion of the VLBI components by a steady and linear function of time supports the assertion that we are in fact observing features ejected from the central engine. This holds for the feature trajectories from the core up to the 1 mas region.

The highly inverted spectrum at the northern end of the 3C 84 jet (see Fig. fig:SPI ) therefore suggests free-free absorption from a foreground absorber. In 3C 84 it could result for example from a circum-nuclear disk or torus Walker00 .

sources.summary: The paper compares its cooling-tail model to observed filaments in NGC 1275, adopting galaxy mass parameters and showing the model matches filament velocities without changing the galaxy mass.

quotes.quote: To illustrate this point, we compare our model to the free fall model used in lim08 in their analysis of observed filaments in the cD galaxy NGC 1275 (Perseus A) located in the Perseus cluster. For simplicity, we follow the approach of lim08 and adopt an analytic model of the mass density and gravitational potential of the form from hernquist90 . The mass density and gravitational potential as a function of radial distance are thus given by: () &= M 2 a 1 (+a)^3 () &= - GM +a where M is the total galactic mass, is the radial distance, and a is a scale length. We also use the same values they deduce from luminosity observations of smith90 and an estimated mass to light ratio, with M=8.3 10^ 11 M _ and a = 6.8 kpc.

Figure fig:cluster1 shows the observational contours of velocity as a function of radial distance from the center of Per A for the outer western filament as shown in Figure 10 of lim08 . In Fig. fig:cluster1 , we have also reproduced the free-fall trajectories used in lim08 , where they include one for galactic masses of M=8.3 10^ 11 M _ (M8b) and M=3.4 10^ 11 M _ (M3b), both starting from a radius of 8.5 kpc. The free-fall model that assumes the M=8.3 10^ 11 M _ mass deduced from luminosity observations is unable to produce a good fit to observations, and hence the mass needs to be tuned to M=3.4 10^ 11 M _ to fit a free-fall model to the observed contours. This tuning of mass and drop height is sensitive to both these factors, mainly due to the rapid acceleration by gravity in free-fall. In comparison, we show the same curve for M=8.3 10^ 11 M _ but using our model(M8c) (equations eq:diff_eq1 eq:diff_eq3 ) which includes the braking effect due to growth from mixing and cooling. This shows the trajectory for a cloud where r_ cl =50 pc, assuming that L/ 100 . We see that if the cloud initially falls from even a radial distance of 15 kpc, it matches the observations well without changing the galaxy mass.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes decades of radio and gamma-ray variability for NGC 1275, deriving core-shift, magnetic-field, jet-launching, and flare-connection constraints for its powerful two-sided jet.

quotes.quote: For this work, we utilise the availability of radio and ray light curves over a few decades of the radio source \ ( NGC 1275 ). We calculate the multiband time lags between the flares identified in the light curves via discrete cross-correlation and Gaussian process regression. We find that the jet particle and magnetic field energy densities are in equipartition ( k_ = ). The jet apex is located z_ 91.5 GHz = ( ) upstream of the 3 mm radio core; at that position, the magnetic field amplitude is B_ core ^ 91.5 GHz = G. Our results are in good agreement with earlier studies, which utilised very-long-baseline interferometry.

\ ( NGC 1275 ), which is the focus of this work, is one of the nearest radio galaxies ( D_ L = 78.9 Mpc, z = 0.0176; Strauss92 ) We assume cold dark matter cosmology with H_0 = 67.8 km/s/Mpc, _ = 0.692 , and _M=0.308 Planck16 . . It harbours a SMBH (M _ BH 9 10^8 M _ ; Scharwaechter13 ), that serves as the central engine of a powerful, two-sided jet. Its core and milliarcsecond (mas) region has been imaged in great detail at many frequencies, both in total intensity e.g. Giovannini18, Paraschos22 and polarisation Kim19 , revealing a limb-brightened structure see for example for a discussion based on MHD simulations Kramer21 and multiple features being ejected almost monthly Paraschos22 .

sources.summary: The paper reports VLBI observations of 3C 84 showing a deflected jet knot, an inverted-spectrum region, and evidence that dense cold ambient gas shapes the parsec-scale jet.

quotes.quote: In this study, we present very long baseline interferometric (VLBI) observations of 3C 84 (NGC 1275), located at the center of the Perseus Cluster. Through monitoring observations with the Very Long Baseline Array (VLBA) at 43 GHz, a jet knot was detected to have been ejected from the sub-parsec scale core in the late 2010s. Intriguingly, this knot propagated in a direction significantly offset from the parsec-scale jet direction. To delve deeper into the matter, we employ follow-up VLBA 43 GHz observations, tracing the knot's trajectory until the end of 2022. We discovered that the knot abruptly changed its trajectory in the early 2020s, realigning itself with the parsec-scale jet direction.

Additionally, we present results from an observation of 3C 84 with the Global VLBI Alliance (GVA) at 22 GHz, conducted near the monitoring period. By jointly analyzing the GVA 22 GHz image with a VLBA 43 GHz image observed about one week apart, we generated a spectral index map, revealing an inverted spectrum region near the edge of the jet where the knot experienced deflection. These findings suggest the presence of a dense, cold ambient medium characterized by an electron density exceeding 10^5 \ cm^ 3 , which guides the jet's propagation on parsec scales and significantly contributes to the overall shaping of the jet.

sources.summary: The paper presents high-resolution SITELLE observations of NGC 1275 filamentary nebula, linking its AGN jets, X-ray cavities, large ionized structure, gas kinematics, and galaxy-scale fountain behavior.

quotes.quote: Here, we target the filamentary nebula that surrounds NGC 1275, the BCG located at the center of the Perseus cluster of galaxies. NGC 1275 has been studied extensively at all wavelengths (see forman_observations_1972 , fabian_wide_2011 , salome_very_2011 , lim_molecular_2012 , nagai_alma_2019 ). The cluster hosts several X-ray cavities that originate from multiple generations of radio jets emitted from the active galactic nuclei (AGN) of NGC 1275 ( 1993MNRAS.264L..25B , fabian_chandra_2000 , fabian_wide_2011 , gendron-marsolais_revealing_2018 ).

NGC 1275 also displays one of the largest filamentary nebulae known with a size of about 80 kpc 50 kpc (e.g. mcnamara_optical_1996 , conselice2001nature , hamer_optical_2016 ). Combined with the proximity of the Perseus cluster, this makes NGC 1275 a target of choice for our understanding of the formation and ionization mechanisms of filamentary nebulae in clusters. The first observations of the filamentary nebula surrounding NGC 1275 by , , and unveiled a high-velocity (HV) feature ( 8200 km/s) associated with a forefront spiral galaxy falling onto NGC 1275 ( 2015ApJ...814..101Y ), and a low-velocity (LV) structure ( 5200 km/s) linked to NGC 1275.

Regarding the formation model explored for this filamentary nebula, it seems that a unifying model as explored by could potentially explain the observed characteristics of the emitting gas observed flux and velocity dispersion measurements obtained through the analysis of the SITELLE observations of NGC 1275. Indeed, regarding the galaxy-scale fountain model explored by we observe similar trends both in terms of kinematics and velocity dispersion between NGC 1275 and Abell 2597.

sources.summary: The paper shows cold molecular filaments in NGC 1275 flowing toward and feeding the circumnuclear disk, linking jet feedback, cooling gas, and AGN fueling.

quotes.quote: We present the results of re-processed CO(2-1) ALMA observations of the cold molecular gas in the central regions of NGC 1275, the central galaxy of the Perseus cluster and hosting the radio-loud AGN 3C 84 (Perseus A). These data show, for the first time, in detail how kpc-sized cold gas filaments resulting from jet-induced cooling of cluster gas are flowing towards the galaxy centre and how they feed the circumnuclear accretion disc (100 pc diameter) of the SMBH. Thus, cooled gas can, in this way, play a role in feeding the AGN. These results complete our view of the feedback loop of how an AGN can impact on its surroundings and how the effects from this impact maintain the AGN activity.

A pivotal object in this respect is NGC 1275, the brightest galaxy of the Perseus cluster and which hosts the radio loud AGN 3C 84 (Perseus A). It is well known for the cavities and ripples in the hot cluster gas created by the jets of relativistic plasma ejected by 3C 84 , as well for its extensive web of optical emission-line filaments which are also detected in warm and cold molecular gas as well as in colder X-ray gas . In the centre of NGC 1275, a circum-nuclear disc (CND) with a radius of about 50 pc of molecular and ionised gas has been found with an orientation such that it is perpendicular to the inner radio jets. The kinematics of the outer parts of this CND suggests that the gas in this region is unsettled, possibly due to gas accreting onto the CND .

sources.summary: The paper follows the parsec-scale jet of the radio AGN 3C 84, describing intermittent lobes, a restarted jet, C3 kinematics, and faster emerging subcomponents.

quotes.quote: 3C 84 is a nearby radio AGN located at the center of the elliptical galaxy NGC 1275 z = 0.0176; strauss92 . It exhibits multiple radio lobes on both sides at parsec scales as the result of intermittent jet activity e.g., pedlar90, wrb94, nagai10, fujita17 . The most recent jet emerged with a radio outburst in 2003 November nagai10, suzuki12 . This is a rare case in which the restart activity of AGN jets was observed.

We have investigated the kinematics of C3 and the new subcomponents that emerged from C3. We have found that the average apparent velocity of C3 has remained constant at _ app =0.259 0.003 over the past 20 years. Since 2017, four new subcomponents ``k1"-``k4" took the appearance of being launched from C3. The apparent speeds of all four subcomponents are larger than that of C3, and ``k2"-``k4" exhibit faster apparent speeds than ``k1".

sources.summary: The paper applies its method to NGC 1275, describing it as the Perseus brightest center galaxy with a large asymmetric optical emission-line nebula and distinct background-modeling behavior.

quotes.quote: NGC 1275 In this section, we apply our methods to the SITELLE observations of the well-studied galaxy cluster http://cdsportal.u-strasbg.fr/?target=NGC 201275 1275 . NGC 1275 is the brightest center galaxy in the Perseus cluster; it hosts a wide range of multi-wavelength astrophysical phenomena ( fabian_relationship_2003 ; fabian_wide_2011 ; krabbe_near_2000 ; hitomi_collaboration_quiescent_2016 ; gendron-marsolais_revealing_2018 ; vigneron_high-spectral-resolution_2024 ). In the optical bandpass, NGC 1275 hosts a large (several dozens of kiloparsecs) and asymmetric emission-line nebula. Unlike the previous test case of 4449, 1275 does not exhibit DIG that needs to be disentangled from the nebular emission; additionally, the background is expected to be relatively stable over the field ( gendron-marsolais_revealing_2018 ; vigneron_high-spectral-resolution_2024 ).

Principal Component Analysis In figure fig:PCAcombined , we show the first ten principal components ordered by explained variance (importance) for NGC 1275. Similar to the PCA results of NGC 4449, the mean emission contains the sky-lines and is a good first-order approximation of the background. Unlike NGC 4449, the principal components do not contain emission lines typical of strong emission lines. In the case of NGC 1275, there is no diffuse nebular emission (the DIG in NGC 4449), so the segmentation algorithm completely blocks out all emission associated with the nebula.

sources.summary: The paper studies NGC 1275/3C 84 as a nearby bright radio galaxy, resolving its sub-parsec jet polarization and finding a limb-brightened spine-sheath structure consistent with toroidal magnetic fields.

quotes.quote: At a luminosity distance of 78.9 Mpc ( z=0.0176 , Strauss92 ) in the Perseus galaxy cluster, \ (NGC 1275) is one of the nearest and brightest radio galaxies in the northern sky, and thus is a prime target to study jet formation. Its parsec-scale jet is characterised by limb brightening Nagai14, Giovannini18 , reaching deep into the sub-milliarcsecond (sub-mas) and contributing to its complex morphology Hodgson18, Paraschos21, Punsly21, Oh22 . Polarisation studies of the source have been carried out in the past Homan04, Taylor06, Plambeck14, Nagai17, Kim19, Paraschos24 , providing insights into the interaction of the jet with the ambient medium, as well as the accretion state of the central SMBH.

Using 22 GHz globe-spanning VLBI measurements of \ we studied its sub-parsec region in both total intensity and linear polarisation to explore the properties of this jet, with a linear resolution of 0.1 parsec. We tested different simulation set-ups by altering the bulk Lorentz factor of the jet, as well as the magnetic field configuration (toroidal, poloidal, helical). We confirm the persistence of a limb brightened structure, which reaches deep into the sub-parsec region. The corresponding electric vector position angles (EVPAs) follow the bulk jet flow inside but tend to be orthogonal to it near the edges. Our state-of-the-art RMHD simulations show that this geometry is consistent with a spine-sheath model, associated with a mildly relativistic flow and a toroidal magnetic field configuration.

sources.summary: The paper detects warm molecular hydrogen in NGC 1275's outer filaments, finds thermal excitation with possible shocks or conduction, and places the extended nebula in the Perseus cluster environment.

quotes.quote: We present clear spectroscopic detections of molecular hydrogen in the outer filaments of the H nebula surrounding the central galaxy of the Perseus cluster, NGC 1275. This implies the presence of warm molecular gas clouds at projected distances up to 24 kpc from the nucleus of the host galaxy, embedded in the hot intracluster medium. The emission-line intensity ratios reveal that the H _ 2 emission is predominately thermally excited with excitation temperatures of 1600 2200 K, suggesting a lack of pressure balance between the molecular component and its surrounding medium. Excitation by stellar UV or by the central AGN is shown to be unlikely, whilst thermal excitation by X-rays or conduction from the ICM or shocks are all possibilities. Evidence for a non-thermal component is found in the spectra of some regions based on a low ortho-to-para ratio.

The large H nebulousity surrounding NGC 1275 has been long known ( Minkowski , Lynds ) and has been studied in a variety of wavelengths. Deep H and X-ray images obtained by the WIYN telescope conselice and Chandra Fabian2003 show spectacular, extended H filamentary structures stretching over 50 kpc from the central galaxy, with corresponding soft X-ray counterparts, deeply embedded in the hot ICM. NGC 1275 lies at the centre of a cluster with strong centrally peaked X-ray emission where the X-ray luminosity implies a mass deposition rate of 300 M _ yr ^ 1 Allen .

sources.summary: The paper observes NGC 1275's extended emission-line filaments with Spitzer, finding warm molecular hydrogen, central cD status, an X-ray peak, and an FRI radio nucleus.

quotes.quote: We present Spitzer high-resolution spectra of off-nuclear regions in the central cluster galaxies NGC 1275 and NGC 4696 in the Perseus and Centaurus clusters, respectively. Both objects are surrounded by extensive optical emission-line filamentary nebulae, bright outer parts of which are the targets of our observations. The 10--37 m spectra show strong pure rotational lines from molecular hydrogen revealing a molecular component to the filaments which has an excitation temperature of 300-400 K. The flux in the 0-0S(1) molecular hydrogen line correlates well with the strength of the optical lines, having about 3 per cent of the H + [ NII ] emission. The 11.3 m PAH feature is seen in some spectra.

The central cD galaxy in the nearby Perseus cluster, NGC 1275, is surrounded by spectacular H filaments which stretch over 100 kpc. These were first reported by Minkowski57 and imaged by Lynds70 , McNamara96 and recently by Conselice01 . The Perseus cluster is the brightest cluster of galaxies (in flux units) at X-ray wavelengths with the emission peaking on NGC 1275 ( Fabian81, Fabian00, Fabian03a ). The nucleus of NGC 1275 also powers an FRI radio source, 3C 84 ( Pedlar90 ).

sources.summary: The paper links NGC 1275 to line-emitting filaments, past star formation, the high-velocity system, and changing nuclear activity inferred from deep Chandra data of Perseus.

quotes.quote: Around the central galaxy in the Perseus cluster, NGC 1275, lies a giant line-emitting nebula Lynds1970,Conselice01 , associated with cool 0.7 keV X-ray emitting filaments FabianPerFilament03 of 10^ 9 mass FabianPer06 . The H emitting filaments appear to be have been drawn out of the central galaxy by the rising bubbles HatchPer06 .

NGC 1275 has long been known to have an A-star spectrum and excess blue light. UV imaging of NGC 1275 by shows a lack of stars above 5 , which means that any continuous star formation (which would have been only 20 ) must have ended about 50 Myr ago. Burst models of star formation at rates up to many 100 100 Myr or so ago are consistent with the UV data Smith92 .

The X-ray data provide insight on the history of the past 100 Myr of activity of the nucleus of NGC 1275. There are hints from the large ripples beyond 100 kpc, and from the the large Northern filament and the presence of many A stars, of a higher level of activity before that, a few 100 Myr ago.

sources.summary: The paper maps cold CO gas in NGC 1275's eastern filaments, finding narrow unresolved structures and interpreting the filament as AGN-feedback-cooled gas partly falling toward the galaxy center.

quotes.quote: We recently found extended CO(2--1) emission from cold molecular gas embedded in the network of H filaments surrounding the galaxy NGC 1275 (Salome et al. \ 2006). We now present CO(2--1) interferometer maps of the eastern filaments, at high spatial and spectral resolutions. The cold molecular gas is detected by the Plateau de Bure Interferometer along the eastern filaments over an extent of 15 '' , or with a projected length of 5 kpc. In our 2.5 '' beam, the main CO filament is mostly unresolved along its minor axis. The multiple peaks along the CO filaments and the low values of the observed CO brightness temperatures imply further unresolved structures that may be giant molecular clouds.

If the filament is on the rear side of the galaxy, then its morphology, total mass, and kinematics is consistent with the scenario proposed by Revaz et al. \ (2008) where the AGN feedback drags warm gas to greater radii, where it undergoes runaway cooling. This process can create the east filament observed in CO, possibly composed of GMCs partly falling toward the center of NGC 1275.

sources.summary: MAGIC observations treat NGC 1275 as the Perseus cluster's central radio galaxy, noting its bright radio emission, blazar-like optical behavior, changing jet inclination, and gamma-ray limits consistent with Fermi.

quotes.quote: The central NGC 1275 radio galaxy is another strong motivation for ray observations of the Perseus galaxy cluster.

Note that NGC 1275 has various characteristics in common with Centaurus A which has also been interpreted as a possible source of ultra-high-energy CRs 2009MNRAS.393.1041H . The NGC 1275 radio galaxy is the brightest radio source in the northern sky. Its jet inclination angle seems to increase from 10^ 20^ at milliarcsecond scales up to 40^ 60^ at arcsecond scales 2006MNRAS.366..758D . Note that NGC 1275 was classified as a blazar by because of its optical polarization, and it has been seen to vary in the optical on time scales of a day 1979ApJ...230L.141G .

The upper limits obtained for the NGC 1275 emission are consistent with the recent detection by the Fermi LAT satellite. In this case a one-zone SSC model for the entire emission is implausible, since the large separation in frequency between the two peaks would require extremely large values of the Doppler factor for the jet 2008MNRAS.385L..98T .

sources.summary: The paper studies NGC 1275 as the Perseus brightest cluster galaxy, linking young outer star clusters and H filaments to radio bubbles in a complex two-galaxy system.

quotes.quote: We present photometry of the outer star clusters in NGC 1275, the brightest galaxy in the Perseus cluster. The observations were taken using the Hubble Space Telescope Advanced Camera for Surveys. We focus on two stellar regions in the south and south-east, far from the nucleus of the low velocity system ( 22 kpc ). These regions of extended star formation trace the H filaments, drawn out by rising radio bubbles.

NGC 1275 is the BCG in the nearby ( z=0.0176 ) Perseus Cluster, the X-ray brightest, cool core cluster. The NGC 1275 system is very complex; discovered it consists of two galaxies, a high velocity system (HVS, 8200 km s ^ 1 ) and a low velocity system (LVS, 5200 km s ^ 1 ). 21 cm observations, line absorption in HI and Ly and continuum absorption in X-rays ekers1976, briggs1982, fabian2000, gillmon2004 show the HVS to lie in front of the LVS.

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 1275 as a nearby FR I radio galaxy whose Fermi gamma-ray flux and spectrum can be explained by cascade emission from a misaligned absorbed blazar.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we focus on the radio galaxy NGC 1275 abdo09a and investigate whether the Fermi spectrum and flux level are consistent with the interpretation of cascade emission from a misaligned, absorbed blazar. NGC 1275 is located at a distance of d = 74 Mpc. It hosts the powerful FR I radio galaxy Perseus A (= 3C 84). The radio jet is directed at an angle of 30^o - 55^o with respect to the line of sight walker94 .

NGC 1275 also hosts a Seyfert-like nucleus with a total line luminosity of L_ BLR = 1.6 10^ 42 erg s ^ 1 zb95 . Fermi observed a ray flux of F(> 100 MeV ) = (2.10 0.23) 10^ 8 ph cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 from NGC 1275, which could be fit by a power-law with a photon index of = 2.17 0.05 abdo09a . However, the Fermi spectrum does seem to exhibit a marked steepening towards higher energies.

sources.summary: The paper reports Fermi LAT gamma-ray variability from NGC 1275, describing it as a nearby FR I radio galaxy with jets, a central black hole, and flare behavior resembling BL Lac objects.

quotes.quote: We report on our study of the high-energy ray emission from the FR I radio galaxy NGC 1275, based on two years of observations with the Fermi LAT detector. Previous Fermi studies of NGC 1275 had found evidence for spectral and flux variability on monthly timescales during the first year of Fermi LAT observations. This variability is also seen in the larger two year data set, during which we observe a large ray flare (June-August 2010). The increased photon statistics from this large flare have allowed the discovery of flux variability from NGC 1275 on the timescales of days.

NGC 1275, also known as Perseus A and 3C 84, is a nearby active galaxy located at the center of the Perseus cluster, at a redshift of z=0.0179 ( red ). Classified as a FR I radio galaxy, NGC 1275 is extremely radio bright, exhibiting clear structure of a compact central source and an extended jet (e.g. radio1 , radio2 ). VLBI and VOSP observations have shown NGC 1275's jets to have an instrinsic velocity of 0.5 0.09 and an orientation angle 30-55 ^ ( walker asada2 ).

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 1275 as Perseus central galaxy surrounded by massive multiphase filaments, whose emission is modeled as collisional heating from particles in surrounding hot gas.

quotes.quote: The brightest galaxy in the nearby Perseus cluster, NGC 1275, is surrounded by a network of filaments. These were first observed through their H emission but are now known to have a large molecular component with a total mass approaching 10^ 11 of gas. The filaments are embedded in hot intracluster gas and stretch over 80 kpc. They have an unusual low excitation spectrum which is well modelled by collisonal heating and ionization by secondary electrons.

A relatively nearby and spectacular example is the filamentary nebulosity around NGC 1275 at the centre of the Perseus cluster of galaxies. This has been extensively studied with recent images of the bright H emission being published by Conselice et al (2001) and Fabian et al (2008). The origin and means of excitation of these filaments remain a matter of debate.

sources.summary: The paper reports MAGIC detection of very-high-energy gamma rays from NGC 1275, describing it as the central Perseus radio galaxy with a nearby AGN and jet whose spectrum likely breaks at tens of GeV.

quotes.quote: We report on the detection of very-high energy (VHE, E>100 GeV) ray emission from NGC 1275 , the central radio galaxy of the Perseus cluster of galaxies. The source has been detected by the MAGIC telescopes with a statistical significance of 6.6 above 100 GeV in 46 hr of stereo observations carried out between August 2010 and February 2011. The measured differential energy spectrum between 70 GeV and 500 GeV can be described by a power law with a steep spectral index of =-4.1 0.7_ stat 0.3_ syst , and the average flux above 100 GeV is F_ =(1.3 0.2_ stat 0.3_ syst ) 10^ 11 cm^ 2 s^ 1 . These results, combined with the power-law spectrum measured in the first two years of observations by the Fermi -LAT above 100 MeV, with a spectral index of 2.1 , strongly suggest the presence of a break or cut-off around tens of GeV in the NGC 1275 spectrum.

NGC 1275 ( z=0.0179 ), the central dominant galaxy of the Perseus cluster, harbors one of the closest active galactic nuclei (AGN), already included in the original Seyfert list se43 . The AGN is a very bright radio source showing an extended jet with Fanaroff-Riley I morphology (e.g. ve94,bu10 ). The optical emission of the nucleus is variable and strongly polarized from 3 to 6 ma79,ma83 , implying that the relativistic jet contributes significantly to the optical continuum an80 .

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 1275 as a complex Perseus BCG with filaments, emission-line nebulae, young star-forming regions, and shells formed by accretion of satellite galaxies.

quotes.quote: The Perseus Cluster is furthermore an interesting target for the identification of UCDs due to its highly unusual brightest cluster galaxy (BCG) NGC 1275. NGC 1275 is clearly not a simple system, and exhibits a highly complex structure, including an extended system of filaments. Along the line of sight, there are two systems minkowski57 : the high velocity system at V = 8200 km s ^ 1 falling towards the centre of the cluster, and a low velocity system at V = 5200 km s ^ 1 . A massive, extended system of emission line nebulae extends from the nucleus of the low velocity system. A number of filaments are seen that contain young, blue star forming regions, which deserve investigation as sites of ongoing massive star cluster formation at the present time in the Local Universe.

The B band imaging is free of these filamentary structures, and exhibits faint shells, indicating that galaxy-galaxy interactions have taken place here. NGC 1275 is a shell galaxy holtzman92,conselice1275 , with these shells formed via the accretion of satellite galaxies which contribute to the mass build up of the BCG.

sources.summary: The paper reports non-detections of linear polarization in AGN 3C 84 (NGC 1275), deriving polarization upper limits, Faraday-screen constraints, magnetic-field estimates, and parsec-scale RM structure.

quotes.quote: We report a search for linear polarization in the active galactic nucleus (AGN) 3C 84 (NGC 1275) at observed frequencies of 239 GHz and 348 GHz, corresponding to rest-frame frequencies of 243 GHz and 354 GHz. We collected polarization data with the IRAM Plateau de Bure Interferometer via Earth rotation polarimetry. We do not detect linear polarization. Our analysis finds 3 upper limits on the degree of polarization of 0.5 and 1.9 at 239 GHz and 348 GHz, respectively.

Assuming depolarization by a local Faraday screen, we constrain the rotation measure, as well as the fluctuations therein, to be 10^6 rad m ^ 2 . From this we estimate line-of-sight magnetic field strengths of 100 G. Given the physical dimensions of 3C 84 and its observed structure, the Faraday screen appears to show prominent small-scale structure, with RM 10^6 rad m ^ 2 on projected spatial scales 1 pc.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 1275 as a VHE FR I-type radio galaxy and dominant cluster galaxy with a very massive black hole, rich environment, moderate viewing angle, and possible BL Lac-type behavior.

quotes.quote: The four radiogalaxies detected so far at VHE, namely M 87 , Cen A , IC 310 , and NGC 1275 , have all been tentatively classified in the literature as Fanaroff-Riley type 1 radiosources, each with some "peculiarities". Apart from that, these active galaxies and their nucleus and jets show very different properties which, based on knowledge already gathered at lower energies, do not suggest any specific prominent common features, except being TeV sources. These VHE galaxies form an emerging class of AGN, which should find its place within AGN grand unification.

One common trend is that the four sources are located in rich environments, and show sign of galaxy interaction or mergers. Both M 87 and NGC 1275 are dominant cluster galaxies with a very massive central black hole. Cen A is presumably a recent merger, in a group of galaxies, and IC 310 is located in the Perseus cluster where radio jets can interact with the intracluster gas. Other common properties could be to have an intermediate viewing angle (about 20 degrees for M 87, 40 for Cen A, between 20 and 50 for NGC1275 and 38 for IC 310), weak or moderate Doppler beaming, and no direct alignment between their radio compact VLBI core and their extended radio structures (grossly misaligned by 70 degrees in M 87 and 45 in Cen A), possibly enhanced by projection effects and inhomogeneous external medium, or related to specific properties of their central engines. Moreover, the detection of some transient BL Lac-type phenomena has been reported or discussed in the literature for the four radiogalaxies discovered up to now at VHE.

sources.summary: The paper discusses 3C 84 as a radio galaxy whose northern counter-jet/jet component has a steep turnover attributed to free-free absorption by ionized gas.

quotes.quote: used VLBA data at frequencies between 2.3 and 43 GHz to study the inner regions of the radio galaxy 3C 84. They found a very steep spectral index of +4 below the spectral turnover in the northern counter-jet component and concluded that it must be due to free-free absorption by ionized gas associated with accretion disk. A similar conclusion had been drawn earlier by based on non-simultaneous data at 8 and 22 GHz. They argued that the surface brightness of the northern component was too low for the inverted spectrum to be due to synchrotron self-absorption.

Similarly, our component 3 of 0316+413 is in the northern jet of the source, where free-free absorption is suggested as the cause for the turnover vermeulen94,walker00 . Based on the low brightness temperature of the northern jet, suggested that the turnover is likely to be free-free absorption. This was confirmed by , who used data between 2.3 and 43 GHz and found the spectral index below the turnover to be +4, much steeper than the expected value of +2.5 for synchrotron self-absorption.

sources.summary: The paper studies NGC 1275 outer-halo star formation, finding young compact clusters and stellar streaks associated with gas filaments that feed halo growth.

quotes.quote: We examine the star formation in the outer halo of NGC 1275, the central galaxy in the Perseus cluster (Abell 426), using far ultraviolet and optical images obtained with the Hubble Space Telescope . We have identified a population of very young, compact star clusters with typical ages of a few Myr. The star clusters are organized on multiple-kiloparsec scales. Many of these star clusters are associated with ``streaks'' of young stars, the combination of which has a cometary appearance.

These young stellar populations appear to be normal in terms of their masses, luminosities and cluster formation efficiency; < 10 of the young stellar mass is located in star clusters. Our data suggest star formation is associated with the evolution of some of the giant gas filaments in NGC 1275 that become gravitationally unstable on reaching and possibly stalling in the outer galaxy. The stellar streaks then could represent stars moving on ballistic orbits in the potential well of the galaxy cluster. We propose a model where star-forming filaments, switched on 50 Myr ago and are currently feeding the growth of the NGC 1275 stellar halo at a rate of 2-3 M _ yr ^ 1 .

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 1275 as Perseus’s central ray-bright AGN, detected by Fermi LAT and MAGIC, with point-like emission, a steep spectrum, and flux that can outshine diffuse cluster emission.

quotes.quote: This cluster also hosts two ray bright AGNs: NGC 1275, the central dominant galaxy of the cluster, and IC 310, a peculiar object that could be an intermediate state between a BL Lac and a radio galaxy. Both were detected with Fermi LAT and MAGIC .
Above 250 GeV, NGC 1275 is detected with a significance > 8 . The shape of the detected signal is in perfect agreement with a point-like object such as expected for an AGN.

The ray emission from the central galaxy NGC 1275 is consistent with a point-like source and no diffuse component is observed. The measured flux is much larger than what is expected from the CR-induced emission, and it could be outshining the latter. However, the NGC 1275 AGN spectrum is very steep and no signal is detected above about 1 TeV, while models expect the CR-induced spectrum to be a flat power-law with no cutoff in the MAGIC energy range .

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C 84 as a bright, low-polarization radio galaxy and calibrator, imaging its southern jet and resolving its inner region into two components.

quotes.quote: 3C 84 3C 84 is a bright radio galaxy with complex structure marked by low polarization (e.g. taylor06, trippe12, plambeck14 ). In this study we actually used 3C 84 as instrumental polarization calibrator as it is (effectively) unpolarized at frequencies 129 GHz; we provide only total intensity images of this source. Figure fig:3c84_imaps displays 22, 43, and 86 GHz images of the source.

Due to technical problems with the 129 GHz receiver we did not obtain a map at 129 GHz. Our observations show the southern jet albeit not the well-known northern counter-jet. At 22 GHz, the source structure is only marginally resolved. At 86 GHz, the inner region of the source is clearly resolved into two bright components.

sources.summary: The paper reports a new northern VLBA feature in NGC 1275/3C 84, interpreting it as a counterjet and discussing jet geometry, gamma-ray implications, and central absorption.

quotes.quote: firstpage - lastpage We report the detection of a new feature at the centre of NGC 1275 in the Perseus cluster, hosting the radio source 3C 84. This feature emerges 2 mas ( 0.8 pc) north of the central core in recent 15 and 43 GHz VLBA images, and seems to be the counterjet to a known radio jet expanding to the south of the core. Apparently, the two jets were born through an outburst around 2005.

The ambient gas density in the direction of the jet is 8 cm^ 3 if the current jet activity is similar to the past average. galaxies: active -- galaxies: individual (3C 84, NGC 1275) -- galaxies: jets -- radio continuum: galaxies NGC 1275 is the central galaxy of the Perseus cluster. It is known as a nearby Seyfert galaxy ( z=0.0176 ) and hosts the compact radio source 3C 84. Cosmic rays accelerated around the central supermassive black hole (SMBH) may be playing an important role in offsetting radiative cooling of the cool core of the cluster fuj12a,fuj13a . The proximity of the object allows us to make detailed observations about the environment around the SMBH and its activities. Early VLBI observations showed that 3C 84 has complicated structures on a scale of pc; a mushroom-like jet is expanding southward from a bright compact core.

sources.summary: NGC 1275 is described as a Perseus-cluster radio galaxy with filamentary gas, interaction with a foreground high-velocity system, and variable GeV emission but limited TeV variability.

quotes.quote: NGC 1275 is a radio galaxy located near the center of the Perseus cluster. It presents a peculiar morphology consisting of extended filament-like gas structures which are likely caused by interactions with intracluster gas. Furthermore, there is a foreground object in the line-of-sight to NGC 1275 which may be interacting with the central galaxy.

The so-called ``high-velocity system'' is thought to be a late-type galaxy whose morphology has been disturbed through interactions with NGC 1275. This high-velocity system is less than 57 kpc from the center of NGC 1275 and spans 25 kpc . Fermi reports a high degree of variability in the emission from NGC 1275 . At very high-energies, however, MAGIC reports only limited evidence of such variability .

sources.summary: The paper analyzes NGC 1275 gamma-ray variability, reporting rapid flares, spectral changes, enhanced X-rays, and hour-scale variability pointing to a compact subparsec jet emission region.

quotes.quote: We report on a detailed analysis of the ray light curve of NGC 1275 using the Fermi large area telescope data accumulated in 2008-2017. Major ray flares were observed in October 2015 and December 2016/January 2017 when the source reached a daily peak flux of (2.21 0.26) 10^ 6 photon cm^ 2 s^ 1 , achieving a flux of (3.48 0.87) 10^ 6 photon cm^ 2 s^ 1 within 3 hours, which corresponds to an apparent isotropic ray luminosity of 3.84 10^ 45 erg s^ 1 . The most rapid flare had e-folding time as short as 1.21 0.22 hours which had never been previously observed for any radio galaxy in ray band. Also ray spectral changes were observed during these flares: in the flux versus photon index plane the spectral evolution follows correspondingly a counter clockwise and a clockwise loop inferred from the light curve generated by an adaptive binning method. On December 30, 2016 and January 01, 2017 the X-ray photon index softened ( _ X 1.75-1.77 ) and the flux increased nearly 3 times as compared with the quiet state.

The observed hour-scale variability suggests a very compact emission region ( R_ 5.22 10^ 14 ( /4) cm ) implying that the observed emission is most likely produced in the subparsec-scale jet if the entire jet width is responsible for the emission. During the active periods the ray photon index hardened, shifting the peak of the high energy spectral component to > GeV , making it difficult to explain the observed X-ray and ray data in the standard one-zone synchrotron self-Compton model.

sources.summary: The paper includes NGC 1275 in a four-AGN Fermi LAT variability analysis with near-continuous detections, large gamma-ray flux variations, linear rms-flux behavior, and shot-noise-like PSDs.

quotes.quote: However, for detection and scientific analysis, only data points which satisfy the significance of 3 , corresponding to a TS value of 9 are considered. The 0.1-300 GeV ray light curves of the four AGNs: the FRI radio galaxy NGC 1275, BLL Mrk 421, FSRQ B2 1520+31, and FSRQ PKS 1510-089 between August 05, 2008 (MJD 54683) to October 05, 2015 (MJD 57300) over a uniform integration time of 3-days with detection significance above/equal 3 are shown in Figure fig:latLc . All the sources are detected over 97 e.g.

A different variability of PKS 1510-089 is due the fact that its flux distribution is bi-lognormal with equal variability in both 2016ApJ...822L..13K and consistent with values inferred for others here. Though the histograms are skewed/lognormal, the rms-flux relation is linear for all the sources with large scatters and a flux offset different from zero at the zero rms.

It should be noted that a Doppler boost ( ) of a few to ten can make the NGC 1275 flux ( ^3/distance^2 ) similar to the BL Lacs and FSRQs studied in this work, though the energetics and dynamics details may be different for each source. In this work, we explored the ray variability characteristics of four LAT AGNs: FR I NGC 1275, BLL Mrk 421, FSRQs B2 1520+31 and PKS 1510-089 with all having a near continuous detection in Fermi LAT over an integration time of 3-days.

sources.summary: The paper finds NGC 1275 has a curved GeV spectrum and unusually rapid gamma-ray variability, with many flaring peaks and sub-day adaptive light-curve bins.

quotes.quote: NGC 1275: The spectrum is shown in Fig. sed_all (a). The spectrum starts to steepen around (1-10) GeV and more complex LP and PLEC models are used to fit the data. The log likelihood ratio test shows that the PLEC modeling when =0.43 0.04 is preferred over the simple PL with a significance of 5.8 . The PLEC model is shown with red color in Fig. sed_all (a) together with LP (blue) and PLEC ( =1 , orange) models.

Among the considered sources, NGC 1275 is unique, where the flux variation in time scales as short as a day can be investigated. Recently, we showed that during the major flares of NGC 1275 in December 2016/January 2017, the source reached a daily peak flux of (2.21 0.26) 10^ 6 photon cm^ 2 s^ 1 and had an e-folding time as short as 1.21 0.22 hours. Even if it was known that the emission from this source was varying in month abdongc1275,kataokangc1275 and sub-week time scales brown , such rapid flux variations had never been previously observed from any radio galaxy in the band.

sources.summary: The paper presents RadioAstron VLBI observations of 3C 84/NGC 1275, resolving its edge-brightened jet near the core and identifying it as a nearby FR I radio-galaxy laboratory.

quotes.quote: Here we report radio interferometric observations of 3C 84 (NGC 1275), the central galaxy of the Perseus cluster, made with an array including the orbiting radio telescope of the RadioAstron mission. The obtained image transversely resolves the edge-brightened jet in 3C 84 only 30 microarcseconds from the core, which is ten times closer to the central engine than what has been possible in previous ground-based observations , and it allows us to measure the jet collimation profile from 10^2 to 10^4 gravitational radii from the black hole. The previously found , almost cylindrical jet profile on scales larger than a few thousand r_ g is now seen to continue at least down to a few hundred r_ g from the black hole and we find a broad jet with a transverse radius of 250 r_ g at only 350 r_ g from the core.

3C 84 (NGC 1275, Per A) is a Fanaroff-Riley type I radio galaxy at redshift of 0.0176. It has a two-sided parsec-scale jet and a strong nuclear emission resolved in a one-sided sub-parsec scale structure . Due to its brightness and proximity 3C 84 is an ideal laboratory to study the jet structure and origin. At the redshift of 3C 84, the angular scale of one milliarcsecond (mas) corresponds to only 0.344 pc.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes NGC 1275 gamma-ray variability, linking early flares to electron acceleration and later larger flares to Doppler-factor or jet-orientation changes.

quotes.quote: We present an analysis of 8 years of LAT ( >0.1 GeV) ray data obtained for the radio galaxy NGC 1275. The ray flux from NGC 1275 is highly variable on short ( days to weeks) timescales, and has steadily increased over this 8-year timespan. By examining the changes in its flux and spectral shape in the LAT energy band over the entire dataset, we found that its spectral behavior changed around 2011 February ( MJD 55600).

NGC 1275 is a well-known galaxy located at the center of the Perseus cluster, at a redshift of z=0.0179 , with an active galactic nucleus (AGN) classified as a Seyfert 1.5. It is one of a few non-blazar AGNs detected in both high-energy (HE; >0.1 GeV) and very high-energy (VHE; >0.1 TeV) rays so far, and is the brightest radio galaxy at GeV energies e.g., 2010ApJ...720..912A, 2016arXiv161102986R .

We presented an analysis of 8 years of LAT data for the nearby radio galaxy NGC 1275. The LAT spectrum accumulated over 8 years is best described by a power law with a sub-exponential cutoff , with the photon index = 1.93 0.01 and the cut-off energy E_ = 12.0 1.7 GeV.

sources.summary: The paper studies the radio galaxy 3C 84/NGC 1275 with VERA, finding C3 moving at 0.27c plus nonlinear motion, and frames 3C 84 as an ideal jet-formation laboratory.

quotes.quote: We present a kinematic study of the subparsec-scale radio jet of the radio galaxy 3C 84/NGC 1275 with the VLBI Exploration of Radio Astrometry (VERA) array at 22 GHz for 80 epochs from 2007 October to 2013 December. The averaged radial velocity of the bright component ``C3'' with reference to the radio core is found to be 0.27 0.02c between 2007 October and 2013 December. This constant velocity of C3 is naturally explained by the advancing motion of the head of the mini-radio lobe. We also find a non-linear component in the motion of C3 with respect to the radio core.

The bright radio source 3C 84 is associated with the giant elliptical galaxy NGC 1275 ( z = 0.0176 ; ), which is a dominant member of the Perseus cluster. Its proximity allows us to investigate not only its large-scale structures, but also the central subparsec-scale region, where the jet nozzle is located, with the high angular resolution provided by observations with Very Long Baseline Interferometry (VLBI). Therefore, 3C 84 is an ideal source to study the formation mechanism of relativistic jets powered by an SMBH and the interaction between the jets and ambient medium in the vicinity of the SMBH (e.g., and references therein).

sources.summary: RadioAstron observed 3C 84 at multiple bands, detected ground-to-space fringes to 8 Earth diameters, resolved its edge-brightened jet, and found an unusually broad, nearly cylindrical restarted jet.

quotes.quote: Nearby AGN key science program is aimed at obtaining images with a spatial resolution of a few to a few hundred gravitational radii in nearby radio galaxies 3C 84 ( D_L = 75 Mpc), M 87 ( D_L = 16 Mpc), and Cen A ( D_L = 4 Mpc). All targets have been observed at multiple RadioAstron bands and at multiple epochs. Ground-to-space fringes have been successfully found for 3C 84 at 4.8 and 22 GHz, and for M 87 at 1.6, 4.8 and 22 GHz.

The early dual-band observations of 3C 84, the central galaxy of Perseus cluster, in 2013 resulted in fringe detections on ground-to-space baselines as long as 8 D_ Earth at both 4.8 GHz (Savolainen et al. \ in prep.) and 22 GHz see also Fig. 3C84 2018NatAs...2..472G . With 27 as fringe spacing, the 22 GHz RadioAstron observations transversely resolved the edge-brightened jet of 3C 84, and allowed measuring the jet collimation profile down to 10^2 r_g from the central black hole. The reconstructed image revealed an initially very broad jet with a transverse radius 250 r_g at merely 350 r_g from the core, which implies that the jet is either rapidly laterally expanding on scales below 10^2 r_g or the outer layer of the jet is launched from the accretion flow and not from the ergosphere of the spinning black hole. The measured collimation profile is almost cylindrical and it clearly differs from the parabolic profiles measured for M 87 2018ApJ...868..146N and Cyg A 2016A&A...585A..33B .

sources.summary: The paper analyzes NGC 1275 UV and X-ray variability, describing it as a Perseus cD radio/Seyfert galaxy whose restarted jet and multiwavelength behavior constrain nuclear emission origins.

quotes.quote: We analyze the ultraviolet (UV) and X-ray data of NGC 1275 obtained with Swift /UVOT, XRT, BAT and Fermi Large Area Telescope over about 10 years to investigate the origin of the nuclear emission from NGC 1275. We confirm that the UV and soft/hard X-ray fluxes gradually increased along with the GeV gamma rays. At times, short-term variations in the UV or soft X-ray spectral regions showed rapid variations correlated with the GeV gamma-rays.

NGC 1275 (3C 84; z = 0.0176: Young1995 ) is a cD galaxy at the center of the Perseus cluster. It is classified as a radio galaxy or a radio-loud Seyfert galaxy. The AGN jet activity of NGC 1275 restarted in 2005, and the radio flux has gradually increased since Nagai2016 . Thus, it provides us with an observational opportunity to study the growth of the AGN jet.

sources.summary: The paper studies 3C 84 as a nearby bright radio galaxy, using GMVA observations to resolve its double nuclear structure and constrain the geometry of its inner jet.

quotes.quote: 3C 84 (NGC 1275) is the radio source at the center of the Perseus Cluster and exhibits a bright radio jet. We observed the source with the Global Millimeter VLBI Array (GMVA) between 2008 and 2015, with a typical angular resolution of 50 as. The observations revealed a consistent double nuclear structure separated by 770 gravitational radii assuming a Black Hole mass of 3.2 10^ 8 M_ . The region is likely too broad and bright to be the true jet base anchored in the accretion disk or Black Hole ergosphere.

We present here a detailed analysis of the GMVA images of a nearby z=0.017559 , strauss1992 radio galaxy, 3C 84. At the redshift 0.0176 the angular scale of 1 mas corresponds to a spatial scale of 0.35 parsec mas ^ 1 , where we adopt for the cosmological constants H_ 0 = 67.7 km/s/Mpc, _ = 0.693 and _ M = 0.307 planck . We adopt 3.2 10^ 8 M_ for the mass of the SMBH in 3C 84 park2017 .

sources.summary: The review discusses NGC 1275 as a Pop. B radio-loud AGN whose rest-frame optical/UV spectra constrain BLR chemical abundance and photoionization parameters.

quotes.quote: We considered the active galaxy NGC 1275 (a.k.a. 3C 84 or Perseus A, a radio-loud AGN with an optical spectrum of Pop. B), and analyzed the rest-frame optical and UV spectra to measure the chemical abundance of its BLR gas. A full account of the analysis is provided by punslyetal18a . The source has a very faint BLR compared to the prominent narrow-line region emission but, once this difficulty has been overcome via a careful multi-component nonlinear fit, it was possible to gain constraints on the target line flux ratios: 0.3 , \ FWHM 5000 \ (implying a spectral type B1 in the MS context) The same considerations however apply to extreme Pop. B, considering that those sources show emission line ratios similar to the NGC 1275. , low [ C iv ] 1549/He ii 1640 and C iv 1549/ \ ratios, high He ii 4686/ .

The fit was carried out using a non-linear, multicomponent, minimum ^2 \ approach with the same technique described in several previous papers e.g., marzianietal10,sulenticetal15 . We exploit here an empirical advantage of the MS: for most of the bins it is possible to consider composites or even a single object as representative of the spectral type marzianietal10 , as (the only exception being perhaps A1; pandaetal19 ) the sources scatter around a well defined average. Recent analyses utilized photoionization computations that cover a six-dimensional parameter space (spectral energy distribution (SED), ionization parameter, density, column density metallicity, micro-turbulence; pandaetal18 ). Exploration of a parameter subspace in density, ionization parameter U, column density, and metallicity with CLOUDY 17.01 ferlandetal17 assuming a carefully defined SED on the basis of multifrequency observations ( a full description is provided by punslyetal18a ) yielded a set of possible solution in agreement with the diagnostic ratios, and with the observed line luminosity: n_ H 10^ 10 cm ^ 3 , N_ H 10^ 23 cm ^ 2 , 0.1 Z_ Z 1 Z_ \ punslyetal18a .

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 1275 as the Perseus BCG where central SSCs and a young, kinematically decoupled spiral disk formed recently around the nucleus.

quotes.quote: SSCs have even been found in large numbers in an elliptical galaxy, NGC 1275, the giant elliptical galaxy at the center of the Perseus cluster; i.e., the Perseus Brightest Cluster Galaxy (BCG). Using the Hubble Space Telescope (HST), star_clus_ID discovered about sixty SSCs within 5 kpc of the nucleus of NGC 1275, for which they inferred masses of 10^5 - 10^8 M_ , unresolved diameters of 15 pc, and ages of several hundred million years or less. A modern HST image of these SSCs, on which the work reported in this manuscript is based, is shown in Figure fig:HST_image . Based on better-focused images taken by the HST, Carlson2001 measured an average half-light radius of 6.2 pc (range from 1--100 pc) for about 175 SSCs (spanning about 6 mag in R band) over a region of size 15 15 kpc centered on the galaxy.

The nature of this central spiral has been studied in detail recently by paper1 (Paper I), who showed that: (i) the disk on which the spiral arms are imprinted is rotating quickly and decoupled from the main body of NGC 1275; and (ii) its stellar population appears to be well-characterised by a single age of 150 Myr (and likely bound within the range 100--200 Myr) and an estimated total initial mass of 3 10^9 M_ (assuming solar metallicity). This age, inferred from spectroscopic measurements that yield a higher accuracy and reliability than those inferred using only colors, is significantly younger than the average age of 500 100 Myr (note that the uncertainty is a combination of measurement uncertainties together with a true age dispersion) derived in Section sec:physical properties for the SSCs projected against the central spiral. Consistent with its younger stellar age, after separating the contribution from the dominant old stellar population comprising the main body of NGC 1275 to the overall colors along the sightline toward the central spiral, we find the central spiral to have B - V 0.2 , which is bluer than the average color of the SSCs.

sources.summary: The paper presents VLBI jet work on NGC 1275, describing it as a bright northern radio galaxy and TeV emitter with slowly moving nuclear jet features and unclear flare associations.

quotes.quote: The radio source \ (NGC 1275; z=0.0176 Strauss92 , see Fig. fig:structure ) is one of the brightest radio galaxies in the northern sky and a TeV emitter . The relatively large viewing angle (estimated to be 11^ 35^ in the nuclear region) at which its jet is moving with regard to our line of sight, combined with the subluminal flow speed of its jet features , allows us to effectively study its jet kinematics, as the Doppler-beaming effect is thus not strong. In the work, presented in , we used over 20 years worth of Global mm-VLBI Array (GMVA) data for \ at 86 GHz, and, in combination with 43 GHz data , we were able to cross-identify at both frequencies and track inside the nuclear region, five jet features, named F _1- F _5 . We fitted a linear regression to the VLBI component trajectories, to calculate their velocity and also back-extrapolate their time of ejection. Our work reveals that the features move with velocities up to 0.1 in the nuclear region, that newer components (after 2016) seem to move faster, and that at 86 GHz, the features move at approximately twice the speed as at 43 GHz.

In our cross-correlation analysis we employed radio light curves at 4.8, 8.0, and 14.8 GHz, obtained at University of Michigan Radio Observatory (UMRAO), at 15 GHz obtained at the Owen's Valley Radio Observatory (OVRO; ), at 37 GHz obtained at the Metsähovi Radio Observatory (MRO), at 230 and 345 GHz obtained with the Submillimeter Array (SMA), and the ray light curve of NGC 1275 ( ) at MeV-GeV energies . For a more detailed description refer to . We do not find a clear relation between the ejection times of jet features and flaring activity in both radio- and ray light curves.

sources.summary: NGC 1275 is the central bright TeV radio galaxy in Perseus and the main MAGIC target; the paper models its surrounding magnetic field to search for ALP spectral signatures and set limits.

quotes.quote: This study analyzes 41.3 hrs of observational data from the Perseus Galaxy Cluster collected with the MAGIC telescopes. We focused on the spectra the radio galaxy in the center of the cluster: NGC 1275. By modeling the magnetic field surrounding this target, we searched for spectral indications of ALP presence. Despite finding no statistical evidence of ALP signatures, we were able to exclude ALP models in the sub-micro electronvolt range. Our analysis improved upon previous work by calculating the full likelihood and statistical coverage for all considered models across the parameter space.

In its center, Perseus hosts a very bright TeV-emitting radio galaxy: NGC 1275 MAGIC:2011wkl,MAGIC:2013mvu, MAGIC:2016jpr,MAGIC:2018xky . NGC 1275 has been extensively sampled by MAGIC, producing a wealth of scientific results because of its intense flaring activities.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 1275 HST photometry as a testbed, describing its cluster system, young blue clusters in the inner halo, and neural-network completeness behavior.

quotes.quote: The photometry of the NGC 1275 cluster system is taken from the raw data described in harris+2020 and the field is shown in Figure fig:ngc1275 . These HST images include exposures in the filters (F475W, F814W) from the ACS/WFC camera, one orbit per filter and three sub-exposures per orbit, with total exposure times of 2436 sec in F475W and 2325 sec in F814W. Previous photometry of the system is reported in harris2023 but the measurements were redone for the present study through the photometric packages in DOLPHOT . The reference image used to detect objects for the photometry was adopted as the deep *.drc F814W image from the MAST Archive, where the three individual exposures were combined through AstroDrizzle . DOLPHOT is a versatile code especially tuned for HST imaging data, and detailed discussions of its many input parameters can be found in, e.g., dalcanton+2009 , monelli+2010 , radburn-smith+2011 , williams+2014 , and cohen+2020 . A number of the parameters are important particularly for photometry in extremely crowded fields. However, as will be seen below, the star cluster population in and around NGC 1275 is not strongly crowded across most of the field and so the choices are less critical.

However, for NGC 1275 an additional feature not seen in most other giant ETGs is the presence of many young blue clusters (color indices 0.6 - 1.2 in Fig. fig:cmd ), which are found in the inner halo and are associated with the gas and dust filaments as mentioned above. These clusters increase the total range of colors in the entire sample of objects, which is a useful feature of the data for our testing purposes.

sources.summary: The paper identifies a compact nuclear X-ray excess in NGC 1275, fits its spectrum with an absorbed power law and iron line at the galaxy redshift, and concludes the nucleus resembles a typical AGN.

quotes.quote: The central excess (within 10'' ) is therefore consistent with being due to the contribution of the unresolved, compact source -- the nucleus of NGC 1275. Spectrum of NGC 1275 Given the XMM-Newton spatial resolution and complicated surface brightness distribution near the cluster core, the precise determination of the NGC 1275 nuclear spectrum is difficult. We used the central 14'' (radius) circle and the 14'' - 28'' annulus as the source and background regions respectively.

It is fairly well fit with an absorbed ( N_H=1.2 10^ 21 2 ) power law with a photon index of 1.65 and a narrow line at an energy of 6.289 keV (with 1 confidence interval from 6.276 to 6.313 keV) and an equivalent width of 165 eV. The assumption that the line is due to fluorescent emission of neutral iron at 6.4 keV implies a redshift of z=0.0176 0.003 , fully consistent with the optically determined redshift of NGC 1275 z=0.01756 .

We conclude that the observed spectrum of the nucleus is consistent with typical AGN spectra. The luminosity of the nucleus derived from the best fit parameters is of the order of 10^ 43 in the 0.5--8 keV range and is subject to systematic uncertainties (at the level of 20 ) due to background subtraction and PSF correction.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 1275 as the giant central Perseus galaxy, surrounded by H filaments that coincide with soft X-ray structures and relate to radio bubbles and cooling-flow gas.

quotes.quote: NGC 1275 in the centre of the Perseus cluster of galaxies, Abell 426, is surrounded by a spectacular filamentary H nebula. Deep Chandra X-ray imaging has revealed that the brighter outer filaments are also detected in soft X-rays. This can be due to conduction and mixing of the cold gas in the filaments with the hot, dense intracluster medium. We show the correspondence of the filaments in both wavebands and draw attention to the relationship of two prominent curved NW filaments to an outer, buoyant radio bubble seen as a hole in the X-ray image.

The giant galaxy NGC 1275 at the centre of the Perseus cluster has long been known to be surrounded by a large H nebulosity (Minkowski 1957; Lynds 1970). The blobs and filaments of the nebulosity are embedded in dense hot intracluster medium and have low ionization. Neither the origin of the filaments or the source of ionization is clear, despite many studies (Kent Sargent 1979; Cowie et al 1983; Johnstone et al 1988; Heckman et al 1989; McNamara, O'Connell Sarazin 1996; Sabra et al 2002).

sources.summary: The paper maps NGC 1275's central molecular gas, finding spatially resolved H2 emission consistent with a clumpy disk around the radio-jet axis and deriving a central black hole mass.

quotes.quote: We present near-infrared integral field spectroscopy of the central kiloparsec of NGC 1275 at the heart of the Perseus cluster of galaxies, obtained with the UIST IFU on UKIRT. The nuclear ro-vibrational 2 emission is spatially resolved and is likely to originate approximately 50 from the active nucleus. The Pa emission is, by contrast, spatially unresolved. The requirements for thermal excitation of the 2 by nuclear X-radiation, its kinematics on sub-arcsec scales, and its stability against self-gravity, together suggest that the observed 2 is part of clumpy disk rotating about the radio-jet axis. The sharp jump in the 2 velocity across the nucleus implies a black hole mass of 3.4 10^ 8 , with a systematic error of 0.18 dex due to the uncertainty in the radio jet inclination.

For several years, NGC 1275 at the centre of the Perseus cluster held the unique distinction of being the only galaxy in a cooling flow cluster for which CO emission from molecular gas had been detected (Inoue et al. 1996; Bridges Irwin 1998 and references therein). The fact that it is also a well-known galaxy merger (as well as having been classified a Seyfert 1, a BL Lac and an FR I radio source) served to obscure any causal connection between the CO and the cooling flow, so the failure to detect molecular gas in other such systems led to a heated debate over the validity of the cooling flow model.

sources.summary: The paper uses Perseus A as an AGN jet calibration source, describing reliable large-scale jet-power estimates, compact flat-spectrum jets, core radio luminosity, and kinetic-power normalization.

quotes.quote: There are three AGN jet sources in the sample used to derive the FP relation by which have reliable estimates of the jet power: M87, Cygnus A, and Perseus A. These estimates are derived from average powers on very large spatial scales in the cases of Cygnus A and Perseus A, while for M87 power estimates exist both for large spatial scales from X-ray cavities, and for jet scales bicknell:96 . We can use these sources to estimate W_0 heinz:05b . We should keep in mind, though, that the sources each have some offset from the fundamental plane relation due to the intrinsic scatter around the plane.

It should also be noted that all of these sources show compact, flat spectrum jets, which is critical if we want to use them as templates for low/hard state jets from Galactic XRBs. The rough power estimates for the three sources are: W_ jet 10^ 45.7 ergs s^ 1 , 10^ 44 ergs s^ 1 , and 10^ 44.5 ergs s^ 1 for Cygnus A, M87, and Per A respectively carilli:96,bicknell:96,forman:05,fabian:02 , while their core radio luminosities are 10^ 41.4 , 10^ 39.8 , and 10^ 41.7 ergs s^ 1 , respectively. Thus, the normalization constants we derive for the sources are W_ 0, CygA 6.3 10^ 37 ergs s^ 1 , W_ 0, M87 2 10^ 37 ergs s^ 1 , and W_ 0, PerA 2.6 10^ 36 ergs s^ 1 , each carrying considerable uncertainty from the estimate of the kinetic power and from the fact that the sources scatter around the fundamental plane.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 1275 as the Perseus cluster central galaxy, describes its multiphase emission-line environment, and studies bright X-ray sources near its nucleus and HVS.

quotes.quote: Here we report on the discovery of a population of 8 point X-ray sources to the N of the nucleus of NGC 1275, which is the central galaxy in the Perseus cluster. All exceed 10^ 39 in X-ray luminosity, and 7 are formally ULX, if they are at the distance of the cluster. The spatial region where they lie coincides with the High Velocity System of NGC 1275. We assume that they are part of that system. We see no other point sources (apart from the nucleus) over the body of NGC 1275 (Fig. 1).

NGC 1275 is embedded in a complex multiphase environment. Optical imaging and spectroscopy first established the existence of two distinct emission-line system toward NGC 1275: a low-velocity component associated with the galaxy itself at 5200 km s^ 1 and a high-velocity component at 8200 km s^ 1 projected nearby on the sky (Minkowski 1955, 1957).

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 1275 as the cD galaxy at the Perseus core, whose nuclear jets inflate X-ray cavities and whose filaments contain molecular, soft X-ray, and OVI-emitting gas.

quotes.quote: The X-ray emission is sharply peaked on the cluster core, centred on the cD galaxy NGC 1275. Jets from the nucleus of that galaxy have inflated bubbles to the immediate N and S, displacing the ICM . Ghost bubbles devoid of radio-emitting electrons, presumably from past activity, are seen to the NW and S.

The central galaxy in the Perseus cluster, NGC 1275, is surrounded by a spectacular filamentary optical nebulosity (see ). This is detected in the emission from molecular hydrogen and CO implying temperatures ranging from several 100 to several 1000 K. Soft X-ray emission is seen coincident with the filaments requiring a significant mass of gas at 0.7 keV (Fig. 9). There is also OVI emission detected by FUSE indicating gas at 5 10^5 .

sources.summary: The paper attributes Perseus hard X-ray excess to the central AGN NGC 1275, supported by variability, spectral fits, and combined BAT, XRT, and XMM-Newton analysis.

quotes.quote: XMM-Newton observations churazov2003 showed that the central region is contaminated by the emission of the AGN hosted by the brightest galaxy in Perseus, NGC 1275. A hard X-ray component has been detected with HEAO 1 by . used BeppoSAX and previous RXTE measurements to prove that this non-thermal component is variable and must therefore be connected to the central bright AGN. reported, using Chandra, the presence of non-thermal X-ray emission in the core of Perseus in correspondence of the radio mini-halo i.e. gis79,git02 . This non-thermal emission, which displays a power-law behavior with photon index of 2.0, seems to exceed the flux of NGC 1275 by a factor 3 sanders04 .
This supports the idea that the hard-tail seen in the BAT spectrum is due to NGC 1275 and not to a non-thermal component originated in the ICM.
It is very likely that the central AGN NGC 1275 is responsible for such emission.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 1275/Per A as the Perseus cluster's central cD narrow-line radio galaxy, estimates its active nucleus, and attributes the variable hard-X-ray power law to that nucleus.

quotes.quote: Its central cD galaxy, NGC 1275, hosts a well-known narrow-line radio galaxy, Per A, which interacts with the intra-cluster gas through its jets and outflows boehringer .

The contribution of the active nucleus in the center of NGC 1275 was also estimated. The spectrum of the AGN could be well-fitted by an absorbed power law with a photon index =1.65 typical of radio-loud AGN and a luminosity of 10^ 43 ergs s^ 1 in the 0.5-8 keV band. In the radio domain, the bright radio source 3C 84 is consistent with the position of NGC 1275. While most of the radio emission comes from the radio galaxy, emission on a larger scale ( 10 arcmin) was also detected in the cluster ( burns , ferettiper ), designated as the Perseus ``mini-halo".

Overall, our analysis reveals very clear signs of variability in ISGRI data, which allows us to conclude with good confidence that the power-law detected in the \ spectrum is due to the presence of the active nucleus in the center of NGC 1275.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 1275 as the X-ray bright active galaxy at Perseus center and models its nuclear/AGN emission as a contaminating hard component.

quotes.quote: An X-ray bright active galaxy NGC 1275, with a radio mini-halo, is located at the cluster center, and non-thermal power-law emission from NGC 1275 was confirmed by past observations (e.g., Sanders et al, 2005).

Nuclear emission from NGC 1275 Nuclear X-ray emission from NGC 1275, which is located at the cluster center, has been reported by past studies, for example, XMM-Newton (Churazov et al. 2003), Chandra (Sanders et al. 2005) and Swift/BAT (Ajello et al. 2008). Eckert et al. (2009) showed that high-energy flux ( > 30 keV) is variable over a time scale of several months with INTEGRAL data, and proved that the origin of the high-energy emission cannot be diffuse. Therefore, we have to estimate the X-ray flux of the nucleus as contamination, to obtain the accurate temperature structure.

However the non-thermal emission from the cluster itself has not been discussed well, because the thermal emission from the cluster core is very bright and furthermore the AGN emission from NGC 1275 must be considered. In this paper, we performed observations of cluster offset regions, by reducing the bright emission from the cluster center. In addition, we considered the contribution of the AGN emission from NGC 1275.

sources.summary: The paper uses 3C 84 as a recurrent radio source with mini lobes, citing a roughly 50 yr injection episode, a 2005 VLBI outburst, a sub-parsec component, and GeV emission.

quotes.quote: Apart from the above two cases, recurrent radio sources are also known to possess mini lobes inside large lobes (e.g., 3C 84, Walker et al. 2000). These mini radio lobes are young and their ages are typically estimated as t_ age 10^ 2-3 yrs (e.g., Fanti 2009, Giroletti Polatidis 2009).
For example, the recurrent mini lobe 3C 84 actually show t_ inj 50 yr (Asada et al. 2006).

We add a comment on the recent VLBI observation of mini lobe 3C 84. It shows the outburst around 2005 and a new component smaller than 1 pc emerges (Nagai et al. 2010). Fermi/LAT also detect GeV ray emission from it (Abdo et al. 2009).

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 1275 as a Perseus-cluster early-type radio galaxy observed by VERITAS, with core-dominated radio emission, Fermi detection, VERITAS non-detection, and later MAGIC VHE detection.

quotes.quote: NGC 1275 is an unusual early-type galaxy located in the center of the Perseus cluster. Its radio emission is core dominated, but emission lines are also seen, making it difficult to classify it according to the Faranhoff Riley (FR) classification . In Fall 2008 the Fermi ray space telescope reported the detection of ray emission from a position consistent with the core of NGC 1275. VERITAS observed the core region of NGC 1275 for about 11 hr between 2009 January 15 and February 26, resulting in 7.8 hr of quality-selected live time. No ray emission is detected above the analysis energy threshold of 190 GeV, resulting in a flux upper limit incompatible with the extrapolation of the Fermi-LAT spectrum.
The detection in Summer 2010 of VHE ray emission by MAGIC has eventually included NGC 1275 among the few interesting radio galaxies for future VHE investigation.

sources.summary: The paper discusses NGC 1275 as a filament-bearing cool-core galaxy whose line ratios need extra heating, whose filaments can be powered by saturated conduction, and may be magnetically dominated.
